title: Narrow Wall Shelf
summary: A four-shelf wall unit from two 1x8s — decorative jigsawed ends, hidden 1x2 hanging cleats, and shelf spacing that's marked once and traced everywhere.
description:
This is the shelf for the wall that's too narrow for furniture: a hallway end, the strip beside a window, over a toilet. Two 1x8 sides, four 1x8 shelves, and — the part that makes it look bought — a decorative curve cut into the top and bottom of each side with a jigsaw. Cut the shape once, then use the first side as the template for the second; traced parts match, measured parts almost match.

The layout rule lives in step 1 and is worth understanding rather than memorizing: keep the decorative cuts within the top and bottom 4", because the top and bottom shelves land at exactly 4" from each end. Cut deeper and your curve collides with a shelf dado line. Mark ALL the shelf positions on both sides while they're clamped together — one layout session, zero mismatched shelves.

The hanging system is the other quiet trick: two 1x2 cleats run under the top shelf and above the bottom one, set flush to the back. They're invisible from the front but give you real wood to drive 3" screws through into studs — a shelf screwed through 3/4" of side grain into drywall anchors is a shelf waiting for a reason to fall.

Brads and glue assemble it; paint finishes it. One 8-footer and one 10-footer, an afternoon, and the awkward wall becomes the useful wall.

tools.note: The decorative end curves. Cut one side, trace it onto the other.
2" brads or screws — either, with glue.
The 3" stud screws through the cleats.

cutList.note: Decorative end cuts stay within 4" of each end.
Flush to the back, under the top shelf and above the bottom one.

steps.title: Shape the sides and mark the layout
steps.body:
Draw your end curve on one side — staying inside the top and bottom 4", because that's where the outer shelves land — cut it with the jigsaw, sand it fair, and trace it onto the second side. Cut and sand that one to the line.

While both sides are clamped together, mark every shelf position across both at once: top and bottom shelves 4" in from the ends, the middle two spaced 10" apart. Shelves are 3/4" thick — mark both edges of each so nothing lands a half-thickness off.

steps.title: Assemble the ladder
steps.body:
Glue and fasten the four shelves between the sides on your layout marks — 2" brads or screws, three per joint. Square the assembly by matching diagonals before the glue sets; a shelf unit assembles as a parallelogram with astonishing ease.

Add the two 1x2 cleats flush to the back: one tight under the top shelf, one on top of the bottom shelf. Glue and brads.

steps.title: Fill, paint, and hang on studs
steps.body:
Fill the brad holes, sand, and paint — a painted build forgives every filled hole.

Find the studs, level the shelf, and drive the 3" screws through both cleats into framing. The cleats exist precisely so this step is boring: real screws, real wood, real studs, and a shelf that holds actual books instead of decorative ones.

images.alt: Narrow painted wall shelf with four shelves and curved ends
